teamObjective.explanation: This is the opening lead, so I already control the trick. My goal is to keep the lead by playing the highest available single, maximizing the chance of winning the trick and continuing to lead out my remaining low cards.

partnerBelief.summary: Based on the public hand count, my partner appears to have only two cards remaining, which suggests they may be close to finishing, but I do not know the identities of those cards and cannot reliably infer their holdings.

opponentBelief.summary: Each opponent appears to hold three cards based on the public hand count, and the opponent_near_finish scenario tag indicates they may be close to finishing. Their specific cards are unknown, and they could hold higher singles, trump-rank cards, or jokers.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Among my four club singles, the 6 is the highest. Leading the club 6 gives the best chance of winning this trick and keeping the lead so I can continue leading my remaining low cards (5, 4, 3) in sequence. Retaining the initiative avoids letting opponents choose combinations that I might be unable to match.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 3 is the weakest card and would very likely be beaten by any higher single, immediately surrendering the lead.
The 4 is still very weak and likely loses the trick, giving opponents control without improving my position.
The 5 is stronger than the 3 and 4 but still lower than the 6, so it has a strictly worse chance of winning the trick while leaving the same remaining hand.

risks: An opponent or partner may still beat the club 6 with a higher non-trump single, a trump-rank 7, or a joker.
If the 6 loses, opponents gain the lead and may force me to pass or play suboptimally.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Leading the 6 is the strongest available single, so it minimizes the chance of losing the trick. Even if it loses, no alternative lead would have fared better.

notes: Beliefs rely only on public hand counts and the new_game event; no hidden partner or opponent cards are asserted as facts.
